---
title: "Is literature-mining exhausted for the functional splice-neoantigen registry?"
subtitle: "Research decision · Issue #1284 · gates #680 cycle allocation · 2026-07-22"
author: "Jin-Ho Lee"
institute: "Splice Neoepitope Pipeline · JH M Lee Lab"
date: 2026-07-22
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [simple, ../../slides/custom.scss]
    footer: "Splice Neoepitope Pipeline · JH M Lee Lab"
    incremental: false
    slide-number: c/t
    progress: true
    chalkboard: false
    code-line-numbers: false
    fig-align: center
    fig-cap-location: bottom
    transition: fade
    background-transition: fade
    width: 1280
    height: 720
# PDF (beamer) render disabled - same Quarto/pandoc longtable issue as the pilot deck.
# HTML reveal.js is the primary delivery; "Print -> Save as PDF" in Chrome for handouts.
bibliography: refs.bib
csl: ../../slides/nature.csl
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

## The question

::: {style="text-align: center; font-size: 1.7em; font-weight: bold; line-height: 1.2; margin-top: 0.4em;"}
We hold 86 functionally-validated splice positives\
but only 1 hard-negative.\
Is more literature-mining the path to a *usable* registry?
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Why now:** each registry-addition cycle (latest: [Issue #1207](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/1207)) is committed by reflex. This deck makes the effort-allocation call explicit - it gates how future [#680](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/680) cycles are spent, and whether the [#585](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/585) calibration pilot waits on the registry or proceeds.
:::

::: {style="font-size: 0.6em; color: #777; margin-top: 0.4em;"}
**Terminology:** "splice-junction-derived neoepitope" = a peptide translated across a tumor-specific neojunction (RNA-level aberrant exon-exon junction). "Functional" = a per-peptide T-cell readout (tetramer / ELISpot / cytotoxicity), as opposed to MS-presentation (the peptide is displayed, but not shown to provoke a T cell).
:::

---

## Where the registry actually stands {.smaller}

`registry.tsv` (n=97), grounded against the file - not recall:

| Class | Count | Detail |
|---|---:|---|
| **Functional positives** | **86** | 82 scorable + 4 non-scorable; **73 direct-provenance** functional assays (patient tetramer, IFN-g ELISPOT, cytotoxicity) |
| **Negatives** | **10** | **only 1 is a hard-negative-true-splice** (`VELEDHVML`, Fisher 2026); 8 candidate-negative + 1 not-splice control |
| **Untested** | 1 | `TEFQTRRAM` (SNAF, presentation-prevalence) |

. . .

::: {.callout-important appearance="simple"}
The registry is **positive-saturated and negative-starved**. The asymmetry (86 : 1 hard-negative), not the absolute size, is the load-bearing fact.
:::

---

## The marginal cycle is dry {.smaller}

[Issue #1207](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/1207) snowballed three human catalogs. **Zero functional positives** - and they failed on *opposite* gates:

| Catalog | Gate 1: splice-derived | Gate 2: functional assay | Landed |
|---|:--:|:--:|---|
| Exitron [@wang2021exitron] | PASS | **FAIL** (MS + computed score) | MS tier ([#681](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/681)) |
| Ovarian [@zhao2020ovarian] | PASS (aberrant) | **FAIL** (MS-eluted only) | MS tier (low-conf) |
| U2AF1 [@biernacki2023u2af1] | **FAIL** (missense) | PASS (cloned TCR + tetramer) | out of scope |

. . .

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Both Gate-2 FAILs are **main-text-confirmed** (Exitron via NCBI PMC8141048; ovarian Discussion defers immunogenicity to "future studies"). The 86 positives were harvested by earlier, higher-yield sweeps; the marginal cycle now returns MS-tier, not functional.
:::

---

## Why the yield dropped: presentation != immunogenicity {.smaller}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 9
flowchart LR
  L[Literature<br/>discovery papers] --> M[MS immunopeptidomics<br/>peptide is DISPLAYED]
  M --> P[Presentation tier<br/>#681 - fills readily]
  M -.rarely.-> F[Functional assay<br/>T cell RESPONDS]
  F --> R[Functional registry<br/>#680 - 86 held, +0 this cycle]
  style P fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6
  style R fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#22c55e
  style F fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#eab308
```

The discovery literature reliably proves **display** (MS) and rarely proves a **T-cell response**. So new catalogs feed #681 (82 entries this cycle alone), while the functional registry is left where earlier sweeps put it.

---

## The binding constraint is the negative class {.smaller}

Even if mining found more *positives*, they are **not the bottleneck**:

::: {.incremental}
- **Calibration ([#585](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/585)) is gated by negatives**, and we have 10 (1 hard). A ranker learns the display->immunogenicity boundary from *both* classes; 86:10 (86:1 hard) is the imbalance that limits it.
- **Literature is separately exhausted for hard-negatives** - [#911](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/911) documented the field-wide hard-negative count at n=1 (`VELEDHVML`); moving it needs wet-lab, not more mining.
- **Mining does not even recover *our* candidates** - #1207 AC-4: all 54 exitron + 103 ovarian peptides vs our n=2156 candidate presenters -> **zero overlap** (ceiling-checked). Disjoint cohorts + HLA; no shared recurrent target.
:::

---

## Options {.smaller}

| | Action | Marginal yield | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(a)** | Keep dedicating cycles to functional-positive literature mining | ~0 new functional positives (this cycle 0/3 catalogs) | recurring analyst cycles |
| **(b)** | Accept positive-saturation; redirect dedicated effort to the **negative bottleneck** (author-data [#1214](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/1214)/[#817](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/817) + wet-lab) | can actually move the hard-negative count | author outreach / wet-lab |
| **(c)** | **Hybrid**: stop dedicated positive-mining cycles, keep the passive Beat-1 watch (still feeds #681 + catches the rare functional positive), redirect dedicated effort to negatives | best expected value | low |

---

## Decision {.smaller}

::: {style="text-align: center; font-size: 1.9em; font-weight: bold; margin-top: 0.1em;"}
Recommended: (c) Hybrid
:::

::: {style="text-align: center; font-size: 1.05em; margin-top: 0.1em;"}
Stop committing *dedicated* functional-positive mining cycles · keep the passive watch · redirect to the negative-class bottleneck
:::

. . .

- **Positives are saturated for now** - 86 held, marginal cycle dry; the passive Beat-1 splice-immunogenicity watch still catches the occasional new one and feeds #681.
- **The next real gain is negatives** - author-data requests ([#1214](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/1214)/[#817](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/817)) and wet-lab; these gate [#585](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/585), positives do not.
- **Routing:** this verdict -> Jin-Ho/PM as the #680 cycle-allocation call; #585 can pilot at 86:10 with the imbalance flagged, rather than waiting.

---

## Caveats - read before citing {.smaller}

::: {.incremental}
1. **"Exhausted" is *marginal*, not absolute** - it means the last cycle's yield was ~0, on a small sample of dedicated cycles (#733, #1207). New primary papers appear; the passive watch stays on.
2. **Positive-saturation is context-specific** - 86 is ample for some uses and thin for a rare HLA / mechanism; a targeted positive gap can still justify a cycle.
3. **The MS tier keeps growing** - "stop dedicated cycles" applies to the *functional* registry, not to #681, which literature feeds for free.
4. **Negative-first is an inference** - that hard-negatives gate #585 more than positives is argued from class balance, not yet demonstrated on a fitted calibrator.
5. **Second decision-tier deck** (pilot: [#592](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/592)); the convention itself is still under evaluation.
:::

---

## References {.smaller}

::: {#refs}
:::
